GitHub
The PMO Cycle

From spectator to
engineering director.

Five steps. Running continuously across every project simultaneously. Here's exactly how Agent PMO breaks the serialisation trap.

Architecture

Two components. One system.

Agent PMO has two parts that work together: a real-time PMO Dashboard that tells you what's happening, and Repo Standards Enforcement that makes every project agent-ready.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ You (PMO Director) │ │ checks dashboard every ~3 min │ └───────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘ │ ┌─────────────▼──────────────┐ │ PMO Dashboard │ │ F# script · launchd poll │ │ CI · PRs · branch · push │ └─────────────┬──────────────┘ │ dispatch ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────┐ │ │ │ ┌─────▼─────┐ ┌──────▼──────┐ ┌──────▼──────┐ │ Agent A │ │ Agent B │ │ Agent C │ │ feature │ │ CI fix │ │ coverage │ └─────┬─────┘ └──────┬──────┘ └──────┬──────┘ │ │ │ ┌─────▼─────────────────▼─────────────────▼──────┐ │ Standardised Repos (enforce-repo-std) │ │ same make targets · same CI · same lint cmds │ └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
The Five Steps

The PMO loop in detail

01

Dashboard scans all repos

The F# script (dashboard/repo-report.fsx) runs every 3 minutes via macOS launchd. It scans every git repository under ~/Documents/Code/ and for each repo collects:

  • Uncommitted file count
  • Current branch name
  • Last commit date
  • Push status (ahead/behind remote)
  • Open PRs via gh CLI
  • CI status via gh CLI
  • Latest GitHub release

It generates a self-contained HTML report at dashboard/repo-report.html — no server needed, open in any browser.

~ launchd · runs every 180 seconds 
$ dotnet fsi dashboard/repo-report.fsx
✓ Scanning ~/Documents/Code/ ...
  Found 24 repositories
✓ Fetching PR status for 24 repos ...
✓ Fetching CI status for 24 repos ...
✓ Report written → dashboard/repo-report.html
  Generated in 8.3s
02

You review the dashboard and prioritize

Open repo-report.html in a browser. The dashboard tells you — at a glance — exactly which projects need attention. No context-switching into each repo to investigate. That's minimal cognitive load.

You're not managing tasks. You're allocating agent capacity. Where's the most value? Which CI failure is blocking a PR? Which project has no activity and needs a nudge?

Key insight: The dashboard is your air traffic control screen. You're the controller, not the pilot. Agents are the pilots.
03

Dispatch agents into standardised repos

Because every repo uses the same structure — same Makefile targets, same CI pipeline, same lint and format commands — an agent dropped into any project already knows how to run, test, and ship. No setup. No hand-holding.

For monorepos or multi-service codebases, git worktrees let multiple agents work on the same repo simultaneously, each on an isolated branch at a different path. A monolith becomes multiple parallel workstreams.

~ git worktrees for parallel agents 
# Agent A — feature branch
$ git worktree add ../myrepo-feature feature/auth

# Agent B — hotfix branch (same repo, different path)
$ git worktree add ../myrepo-hotfix hotfix/api-timeout

# Both agents work independently — zero conflicts
✓ Parallel workstreams active
04

TMC conducts cross-project orchestration

Too Many Cooks (TMC) is project-level agent orchestration. When multiple agents are active across multiple projects simultaneously, TMC prevents conflicts and manages dependencies.

Agents using TMC follow a strict protocol:

  • Register immediately with a descriptive name and intent
  • Lock files before editing — never touch a locked file
  • Broadcast plans before starting work
  • Release locks immediately when done
  • Check for messages every few minutes

TMC sequences, not just parallelises. If a feature branch needs passing tests before a PR opens, TMC enforces that. If two projects share a library version, TMC knows.

05

Review and ship

By the time code reaches you, it has passed every automated quality gate: lint, type check, format, unit tests, integration tests, coverage thresholds, and CI.

You're not the first line of defence — you're the final gate. Human review is for intent, architecture, and edge cases — not for checking whether the tests pass.

Coverage thresholds are monotonically increasing. They never regress. Each PR must maintain or improve coverage. Agents fight to meet the bar, not to lower it.

Ready to direct your own engineering organisation?

Set up Agent PMO in minutes. It's open source and free.
